source.title: A non-reward attractor theory of depression

confidence_rationale: This is the only paper in this set that explicitly uses attractor state language for depression and proposes a computational model in which the balance between reward and non-reward input -- not absolute non-reward -- determines which attractor state wins. The reciprocal inhibition architecture directly implies that sufficient reward input prevents the non-reward attractor from engaging. Both reducing non-reward and increasing reward are valid interventions. Confidence limited by: model operates at neural circuit level (OFC), not environmental resource density; ratio threshold not parametrically specified; does not distinguish hazard/harm from non-reward.
failure_signatures: Model operates at neural circuit level -- does not specify environmental resource density as the parameter.
Ratio threshold is not parametrically defined in the model.
Harm and non-reward are not cleanly distinguished -- hazard exposure and reward absence are treated as functionally equivalent inputs.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Depression is an attractor state in the lateral OFC non-reward system, maintained by reciprocal inhibition with the medial OFC reward system. The non-reward attractor engages when reward input is insufficient to maintain the reward attractor. Antidepressants may work by facilitating the reward attractor, which quashes the non-reward attractor via reciprocal inhibition.
mapping.ree_translation: The Rolls model is structurally equivalent to Q-034 at the neural circuit level: attractor state engagement is determined by the balance of reward and non-reward input, not absolute non-reward alone. Sufficient reward input maintains the healthy attractor. This validates the ratio logic: both reducing hazard (non-reward input) and increasing resource access (reward input) are effective because they shift the same balance parameter.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The model uses reward/non-reward circuit inputs as the key variables; REE uses benefit_exposure and harm_exposure in the environment. These are related but not identical -- the circuit model does not address how environmental resource density maps to OFC input strength. The model does not specify the parametric threshold between attractors.
mapping.source_context: Computational model of OFC circuits using integrate-and-fire neurons; human and animal neuroimaging evidence reviewed; applies to MDD and bipolar disorder

confidence_components.notes: Strong mechanistic model from a senior computational neuroscientist. Mapping fidelity is good at the structural level (attractor state, balance of inputs) but requires an inferential step from OFC circuit inputs to environmental resource/hazard densities. Transfer risk reflects this gap plus the fact that the specific ratio threshold is not specified.
